Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to dedicate chunks of time, maybe 35 hours a week total, but it never felt organized. I'd often find myself studying late at night or in random bursts when anxiety kicked in. With five online courses going at once and limited resources at home, I was constantly juggling and rarely felt like I had a real system in place.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have these intense periods right before deadlines where I'd cram everything in, then quieter stretches where I'd barely touch the material. Life stuff kept interfering too—work, family obligations, the usual chaos that comes with being 28 and not having much financial cushion. Regular schedules just weren't realistic for me.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline or realizing I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd get a notification reminder and that would push me to log in. I wasn't one of those students who checked in daily out of habit—it was more reactive, like putting out fires. The anxiety would build up and then I'd finally force myself to see what I was behind on.
Q4: There were brief moments, maybe a week or two, where I got into a rhythm of checking in every couple of days. It happened when I was doing better mentally and had fewer outside pressures. But those periods never lasted long. Something would come up, my stress would spike, and the routine would fall apart again.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could watch them, pause, rewind, and actually absorb the information at my own pace. I logged about 175 minutes of video time, which helped me understand concepts better than just reading. When instructors used diagrams or visual examples, that's when things really clicked for me.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums—I only posted twice the whole course. They felt like extra work that wouldn't directly help my grade, and I was already stretched thin. Reading materials I'd often skim or skip entirely if there wasn't a clear visual component. When you're anxious and overwhelmed, you start triaging what seems absolutely necessary versus what feels optional.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. If I could see how it would help me complete something that was graded, I'd engage with it. Anything that felt abstract or like busywork got pushed aside. With my anxiety levels, I needed to feel like every hour I spent was actually moving me forward toward passing.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones had this weight of consequence attached—skip them and your grade suffers. Optional stuff, even if interesting, always got sacrificed when time got tight. There was also this mental calculation where I'd think, 'If it's not required, the instructor must not think it's essential,' so I'd deprioritize it.
Q9: Absolutely. I don't have reliable internet at home, so I'd download everything I could when I had access—videos, PDFs, lecture slides. Then I'd study from those materials offline for hours. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, sitting with printed notes or watching downloaded videos on my phone during commutes.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing downloaded materials or working through problems in a notebook, none of which would show up anywhere online. I'd also study with a couple of peers in person, talking through concepts and quizzing each other. The LMS only captured a fraction of what I was actually doing to learn.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online to make sure it downloaded correctly, then watch it two or three more times offline. Same with readings—I'd open them once on the LMS, save them, and then study from the saved files. My actual engagement with the material was way higher than my login patterns would suggest.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof, I was behind in multiple courses, and I started feeling like I'd never catch up. There were days I couldn't even bring myself to log in because seeing all the overdue items made me spiral. The CBT sessions helped eventually, but there were definitely weeks where I was barely functioning academically.
Q13: Constantly. When I was exhausted or stressed about money or dealing with personal stuff, the LMS became this thing I'd avoid. Fatigue made it hard to focus on videos even when I watched them. Competing responsibilities meant I'd often choose sleep or work over coursework. Confusion about assignment requirements sometimes paralyzed me into doing nothing at all.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd log in, click through some materials, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd have a video playing but be thinking about bills or arguments or whatever else was weighing on me. Those sessions felt like going through the motions—technically present but not actually learning anything meaningful.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made it worse—the bigger something felt, the more I'd avoid it. It's like this paralysis where you know you need to do something but the stress of starting feels unbearable. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd see something was due in two days and suddenly find the motivation to engage. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically around due dates. It's not healthy, but that external pressure was often the only thing that could cut through my avoidance patterns.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback motivated me to keep going, made me feel like maybe I could actually do this. But critical feedback sometimes sent me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. With my anxiety, I'd catastrophize negative comments and it would take time to bounce back and re-engage.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having five courses simultaneously made it overwhelming. When modules were clearly laid out with visual timelines, I could manage better. But when things felt scattered or when multiple courses had conflicting deadlines, I'd shut down. More structure would have helped me stay on track despite my tendency to drift.
Q19: Clear visual organization made engagement easier—when I could see at a glance what was due and what I'd completed. What made it harder was when important information was buried in text-heavy pages or when navigation felt confusing. Also, not having offline-friendly options built in made things difficult given my internet situation.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—logging in frequently out of anxiety, clicking around, checking things multiple times, but then being too overwhelmed or confused to actually complete the work. High activity might reflect stress and uncertainty rather than productive engagement. Sometimes you're just spinning your wheels.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline that never showed up in the logs. Someone could have low LMS activity but be studying hard from downloaded materials, textbooks, or study groups. Performance on exams can come from sources the system never sees. The LMS isn't the whole picture.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the invisible work. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just clicking around anxiously. They definitely miss offline study, mental health struggles, and the real reasons behind the numbers.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and wrestling with the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're thinking about course concepts even when you're not logged in, when you're curious rather than just compliant. For me, real engagement happened in quiet moments with downloaded materials, not necessarily when I was clicking through the LMS.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels, my financial stress, or how hard I was fighting just to stay enrolled. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline or the peer conversations that helped me understand difficult concepts. It also wouldn't show that despite everything working against me, I had enough resilience to push through and earn a Merit. That matters.
